coredata.dc:title: Carbon nanodots as dual role of crosslinking and reinforcing chloroprene rubber
coredata.dc:creator: Kong, Lingmin
Zhu, Yong
Huang, Guangsu
Wu, Jinrong
coredata.dc:description: The environmental-friendly and sustainable development is a top concern of rubber industry. Here we report a green method to fabricate a class of mechanically robust chloroprene rubber (CR) by employing nitrogen doped carbon nanodots (N-CDs) as both crosslinker and reinforcing nanofillers. The N-CDs are prepared by a green bulk high-temperature carbonization method on large-scale and can crosslink CR by nucleophilic substitution. The vulcanization kinetics are studied by curemeter. Moreover, the covalent crosslinks result in strong interfacial interaction between N-CDs and CR, enabling exceptional reinforcement of the rubber matrix. Consequently, N-CDs crosslinked CR manifests excellent mechanical properties with a tensile stress up to 30 MPa and an elongation at break up to 1353%. With no carcinogenic and environmentally toxic additives included, this work provides a new strategy to prepare high-performance green rubber composites without pollution.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chloroprene rubber
Carbon nanodots
Green
Vulcanization

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Dispersion state of N-CDs in DVCR
The interfacial interaction and network structure of DVCR

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Aerospace Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) FT-IR spectra of N-CDs, citric acid and urea. (b) WAXD pattern of N-CDs. (c) 2D Fluorescence spectrum of N-CDs. (d) XPS spectrum of N-CDs and high-resolution spectra of C1s (e), N1s (f).
A schematic illustration for possible structure of N-CDs.

(a) Curing curves of CVCR and pure N-CDsx/CR with different loading of N-CDs. (b) Curing curves of CVCR and DVCR-x with different loading of N-CDs. (c) Swelling ratio and crosslinking density of CVCR and DVCR-x with different loading of N-CDs. (d) Zeta potential of different concentration N-CDs solutions. (e) Representative plotting of derivative of α as a function of α for the DVCR-x at 160 °C. The lines are the results of predicted from Eq. (6).
SEM images of DVCR: (a)&(b) DVCR-3, (c)&(d) DVCR-6, (e)&(f) DVCR-9.
(a) Storage modulus and (b) loss factor versus temperature curves of CVCR and DVCR-x with different loading of N-CDs.
Stress-strain curves of CVCR and DVCR-x with different loading of N-CDs.
Schematic of vulcanization and reinforcement mechanism in DVCR.
Formulae of CVCR, DVCR-x and pure N-CDsx/CR.

head.ce:title: Carbon nanodots as dual role of crosslinking and reinforcing chloroprene rubber

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Polymer Materials Engineering, College of Polymer Science and Engineering, Sichuan University, Chengdu, 610065, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Polymer Materials Engineering
College of Polymer Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Polymer Materials Engineering, College of Polymer Science and Engineering, Sichuan University, Chengdu, 610065, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The environmental-friendly and sustainable development is a top concern of rubber industry. Here we report a green method to fabricate a class of mechanically robust chloroprene rubber (CR) by employing nitrogen doped carbon nanodots (N-CDs) as both crosslinker and reinforcing nanofillers. The N-CDs are prepared by a green bulk high-temperature carbonization method on large-scale and can crosslink CR by nucleophilic substitution. The vulcanization kinetics are studied by curemeter. Moreover, the covalent crosslinks result in strong interfacial interaction between N-CDs and CR, enabling exceptional reinforcement of the rubber matrix. Consequently, N-CDs crosslinked CR manifests excellent mechanical properties with a tensile stress up to 30 MPa and an elongation at break up to 1353%. With no carcinogenic and environmentally toxic additives included, this work provides a new strategy to prepare high-performance green rubber composites without pollution.

ce:section.ce:para: Since Goodyear found sulfur could vulcanize natural rubber in 1844, rubbers have proven their worth in diverse technological applications, due to their entropic elasticity, high deformability and low stiffness. Vulcanization crosslinks linear polymer chains into three-dimensional (3D) network, which provides better mechanical performance and shape stability to ensure long-term durability and reliability under various ambient conditions [1,2]. Like many classical chemical reactions, various additives such as activator and accelerator are indispensable for improving the vulcanization efficiency and mechanical properties. However, these additives usually generate toxic volatile organic compounds (VOCs), leading to unpleasant smelling during vulcanization and practical applications. Meanwhile, some vulcanizers and additives are carcinogenic and environmentally toxic [3]. For example, ethylene thiourea (ETU) is a strong cancerogen and zinc oxide (ZnO) has severe toxicity to aquatic organisms, while both of them are widely used as vulcanizers or additives [4]. Therefore, developing facile and environmentally friendly crosslinking agents is highly demanded yet challenging for rubber industry.

One solution is to find new non-toxic vulcanizers and accelerators, thus some researchers shift their attention to the natural compounds. Das and co-workers [5] found that l-cystine derivative could replace ETU to crosslink CR with improved mechanical properties. Zhang and co-workers [6] prepared a robust, recyclable epoxidized EPDM crosslinked by sebacic acids. However, these materials still need to be compounded with fillers to improve the mechanical performance toward engineering applications. Traditional fillers such as carbon black often require large loading, which is environmentally unfriendly and energy consuming. Thus, a promising strategy is to develop nanofillers acting as a dual role of chemical crosslinker and reinforcing filler [7–10]. However, neither traditional fillers such as carbon black and silica, nor advanced nanofillers like carbon nanotube and graphene can crosslink rubbers due to their chemical inertness. To address this issue, surface treatment with functional groups is always needed through some typical reactions such as epoxy/carboxy reaction, Si–H/vinyl hydrosilylation and TESPT/sulfur reaction [11–15]. These modifications frequently need abundant organic solvent and toxic reagents, and moreover the resulting nanoparticles usually show low crosslinking efficiency. Unlike intentional surface modification, we found that the intrinsic oxygenic groups can undergo homolytic cleavage upon heating, and release radical to crosslink styrene-butadiene rubber (SBR) with quite high efficiency. This work unravels the promising potential of heteroatom-doped carbon-based nanoparticles as a novel class of crosslinker [16].

Chloroprene rubber (CR), the first synthetic rubber, is still widely used because of its high mechanical properties and chemical stability. The vulcanization mechanism of chloroprene rubber is quite different from that of other diene rubbers. Due to the electronic effect of Cl atoms, sulfur cannot vulcanize CR. Instead, CR are usually vulcanized with ETU and ZnO by reacting with the 1,2-units (allyl chloride) on chloroprene chains [17–19]. Meanwhile, the allyl chloride can undergo nucleophilic substitution with amine groups. However, diamine is an ineffectual vulcanizer compared with ZnO and ETU [2]. Therefore, ZnO and ETU are still the key components in the vulcanization of CR with good mechanical performance, although they are unfriendly to environment. In this context, we are obligated to develop alternative vulcanizers for CR.

In this report, we describe a new green strategy to crosslink CR with good mechanical performance using carbon nanodots as both crosslinker and reinforcing nanofiller. Carbon nanodots, a new member of carbon materials, can be easily doped by heteroatom via choosing the corresponding precursors [20,21]. We herein synthesize nitrogen doped carbon nanodots (N-CDs) by a bulk high-temperature carbonization method on a large scale. The abundant nitrogen-containing functional groups on the surface of N-CDs allow them to act as chemical crosslinker due to the reactive amine groups (Scheme 1 ). Meanwhile, hydrogen bonds (H-bond) between Cl atom and functional groups doped on N-CDs can reinforce composites to offset poor mechanical performance caused by low crosslinking density generated by amine vulcanization (Scheme 1). As a result, the N-CDs crosslinked CR even shows higher mechanical properties than the ZnO crosslinked CR.

CR was purchased from Chongqing Changshou Co., Ltd (China). Citric acid (98%), urea (98%) and light magnesium oxide (MgO) were purchased from Adamas reagent Co., Ltd (China). ETU, ZnO, stearic acid (SA) and antioxidant NBC were provided by Sichuan Haida Rubber Group Co., Ltd.

For preparation of N-CDs, firstly, the mixture of 8 g citric acid and 2.4 g urea which were added into a 250 ml ceramic crucible was heated at 200 °C with mechanical stirring (500 rpm). The mixture was melted quickly and became yellow fluid. The occur of massive bubbles indicated the beginning of carbonization. After 15 min, the bubbles slowly disappeared, then the stirring was removed. The mixture was then heated at 200 °C for another 10 min in order to achieve high carbonization level. Finally, the mixture turned into black solid, and it was taken out and cooled under ambient condition. The black solid was grinded and stored in a desiccator.

N-CDs crosslinked CR (DVCR) and conventional ZnO/ETU crosslinked CR (CVCR) were prepared by traditional melt blending method. For DVCR, other curing agents were added into CR in an open two-roll mill (SK–160B, Shanghai Huaxiong Machinery Co., Ltd., China) at room temperature with a friction ratio of 1:1.2 and nip gap of ~1 mm firstly. Then, a certain amount of N-CDs was added into the mixture in this open two-roll mill. The samples were named as DVCR-x, where x denotes x phr (parts per hundred of rubber) of N-CDs. For CVCR, the ZnO and ETU were added into CR after addition of other curing agents. Then, all of the samples were hot-pressed in a mold at 160 °C for the optimum vulcanizing time which was determined by a curemeter. Control samples pure N-CDsx/CR, where x denotes x phr of N-CDs, were prepared similarly without addition of MgO during compounding. The formulae of CVCR, DVCR-x and pure N-CDsx/CR are listed in Table 1 .
The detailed characterizations of N-CDs are shown in supporting information.

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) was performed on a FEI Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN. For DVCR, ultrathin sections were obtained using a Leica Ultracut UCT ultramicrotome with a diamond knife cooled by liquid nitrogen to −100 °C. SEM was performed on FEI Apreo S HiVoc.

Curing properties were examined by Ruidayuchen MDR-A curemeter at 160 °C. After pre-heating the discs of ODR to a temperature of 160 °C, 5 g of the samples was inserted between the two discs and the torque was monitored as a function of time. The equilibrium swelling ratio is defined as w 1/w 2, where w 1 is the total weight of rubber and solvent in the swollen sample and w 2 is the weight of rubber in the deswollen rubber. Based on the Flory-Rehner equation, the crosslinking density can be determined [22]:
−
ln
1 −
v r
+
v r
+ χ
v r 2
=
V 0
n
v r
1 / 3
−
v r
/ 2
( 1 )
where v
r is the volume fraction of the rubber in the swollen mass, V 0 is the molar volume of the solvent (106.2 cm3 for toluene), n is the number of active network chain segments per unit of volume (crosslinking density), and χ is the Florye-Huggins polymer-solvent interaction. The value of χ for CR in toluene is 0.393 [19,23]. The value of v
r is attained according to:
v r
=
w 2
/
ρ 2
w 2
/
ρ 2
+
w 1
-
w 2
/
ρ 1
2
where ρ 1 and ρ 2 are the densities of the solvent and the rubber, respectively.

Dynamic mechanical tests were performed on a DMA Q800 (TA instruments) at a frequency of 1 Hz from −80 °C to 30 °C by using a tensile mode at an oscillation amplitude of 20 mm. The heating rate was 3 °C/min. The dimensions of the rectangle sample strips were 20 × 4 × 1 mm. Mechanical properties were performed on a universal testing machine (Instron 5966) at room temperature with a cross-head speed of 500 mm min−1. The specimen was a dumbbell shaped thin strip with dimensions of 25 × 4 × 1 mm. At least three specimens have been tested, and the average data were adopted.

Traditional synthesis methods of CDs, like hydrothermal method and microwave method, have some disadvantages such as low yield and large amount of organic solvent and water consumption. Moreover, the milligram level yield is not sufficient for our large-scale engineering applications. Thus, we choose bulk high-temperature carbonization method which overcomes the above disadvantages, as it is green and one-pot synthesis, and can be used for large-scale preparation. In this method, the organic precursors are heated above the carbonization temperature to make it carbonize into the carbon dots without any solvent [20,24].

TEM is used to measure the morphology of the synthesized carbon nanodots, as shown in Fig. S1. The TEM image shows quasi-spherical dots with a calculated particle size of 9.5 ± 2.8 nm and a Gaussian particle size distribution ranging from 4 to 16 nm, proving the successful synthesis of carbon nanodots [25]. XRD profile displays a wide peak at 2θ = 23.4°, corresponding to the (002) interlayer spacing distance of 0.34 nm (Fig. 1 (b)), which is very similar to that of bulk graphite, showing that N-CDs have a graphite-like structure [26]. The optical properties are measured by 2D Fluorescence spectrum, as shown in Fig. 1(c). The solution of N-CDs has a wide emission from 400 nm to 565 nm and a maximum blue emission, which is dependent on excitation wavelength similar to many other carbon nanodots [27].

The structure and surface properties of N-CDs are vital to crosslink and reinforce CR. The FTIR spectroscopy was used to characterize the surface and structure of N-CDs and the results were compared to citric acid and urea precursors, shown in Fig. 1(a). The N-CDs show typical two peaks in 1710 cm−1 and 1780 cm−1 which belong to CO stretching mode of carboxylic groups. Compared to same peaks in the spectroscopy of citric acid precursor, the peak at 1710 cm−1 of N-CDs exhibits significant higher intensity than the peak at 1780 cm−1, indicating that almost all of the carboxylic groups are involved in H-bonds. Moreover, N-CDs show the amide vibrational mode Ⅰ and Ⅱ at 1653 cm−1 and 1560 cm−1 like urea precursor. More characteristic absorption peak such as N–H, O–H (2900-3500 cm−1) and C–N, C–O (1438, 1402, 1341 cm−1) are observed [28]. Therefore, FTIR spectroscopy demonstrates that the surface of the N-CDs bears a large number of functional groups such as carboxyl groups and amino groups.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) is used to gain more details of the surface properties of CDs. Fig. 1(d) shows the XPS survey curve of N-CDs. Three predominant peaks due to the binding energies of C1s, N1s and O1s are observed at 233eV, 400eV and 534eV, indicating that the main components of N-CDs are carbon, nitrogen and oxygen. The ratio of these elements is 44.3:12.9:42.8 (C: N: O), suggesting pretty high doping concentration of nitrogen in CDs. Fig. 1(e)&(f) show the high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s and N1s. The C1s peak can be divided into five convolutions, including C–C/CC (284.3eV), C–N (285.1eV), C–O (285.9eV), CO (288.1eV) and O–CO (289.2eV). The N1s peak can be deconvoluted into three peaks, corresponding to sp3 hybrid nitrogen (H–N, 401.3eV), pyrrolic typical nitrogen (C3–N, 400.5eV) and pyridinic nitrogen (C–N–C/CN, 399.8eV) in N-CDs [29]. These results prove that N-CDs obtained by bulk carbonization method have high nitrogen doping content and abundant nucleophilic sites on the surface, so that N-CDs can be used as cross-linker for CR. Fig. 2 illustrates the possible structure of N-CDs.

The abundant nitrogen-containing functional groups doped on the surface of N-CDs can undergo nucleophilic substitution with 1,2-units of CR chains to form interfacial crosslinks. To illustrate the vulcanization kinetics of carbon nanodots, the curing curves of the pure N-CDs/CR composites without the addition of MgO are shown in Fig. 3 (a), and the results are compared with those of pure CR and CVCR. The maximum torque (MH) and the difference between maximum and minimum torque (ΔS) of the vulcanization curves can reflect the crosslinking density and thus are regarded as important paraments of vulcanization process [30,31]. Obviously, pure CR shows very small MH and negligible ΔS though its curing curve increases slightly due to aging. This suggests that the pure CR cannot be vulcanized at 160 °C. By contrast, the pure N-CDs/CR composites show evident increase in torque during vulcanization, suggesting that N-CDs have the ability to crosslink CR. However, compared with CVCR, the pure N-CDs/CR composites show significant lower MH and ΔS. These results indicate that the activity of interfacial reaction between N-CDs and CR is quite low. Moreover, it is very interesting that increasing the N-CDs loading decreases the MH and ΔS of the pure N-CDs/CR composites. Such abnormal behavior can be attributed to surface property of N-CDs. In addition to the large number of nitrogen-containing basic groups on the surface of N-CDs, there are also abundant oxygen-containing acidic groups such as carboxyl groups. The oxygen-containing acidic groups may prevail over the nitrogen-containing basic groups, thus N-CDs are negatively charged. To prove this, the zeta potentials of N-CDs solutions with various concentrations were measured, and the results are shown in Fig. 3(d). Indeed, the zeta potential of N-CDs is negative and shifts to the more negative direction with the increase of N-CDs concentration. Therefore, with the increase of N-CDs loading, the acidity of the system increases; this hinders the nucleophilic substitution, leading to the decrease of MH and ΔS.

Since the activity of interfacial reaction between N-CDs and CR is low, MgO is added into the N-CDs vulcanization system, and the resulting samples are denoted as DVCR-x. Unlike ZnO, MgO has much lower reactivity and thus cannot be used to crosslink CR. Instead, MgO is usually used as an acid acceptor to offset hydrochloric acid released by nucleophilic substitution during vulcanization [19]. The curing kinetics of DVCR-x are shown in Fig. 3(b). With presence of MgO, DVCRs show much higher MH and ΔS, indicating that vulcanization efficiency of N-CDs is significantly improved by MgO. Moreover, with the increase of N-CDs loading from 3 phr to 9 phr in the DVCR composites, MH increases from 10.1 dN m to 12.1 dN m, and meanwhile the ΔS increases from 7.8 dN m to 9.9 dN m, indicating that the crosslinking density of DVCR increase. However, MH of DVCR-9 with the highest loading of N-CDs is still lower than that of CVCR, thus the crosslinking density of DVCR-9 is lower.

This conclusion can also be confirmed by equilibrium swelling measurements in toluene, as shown in Fig. 3(c). According to the Flory-Rehner equation, the increase of N-CDs loading raises the crosslinking density from 6.39 × 10−5 mol/cm3 to 7.01 × 10−5 mol/cm3.

In addition to MH, ΔS and crosslinking density, some other paraments are significant to measure the curing properties of the rubber, such as t 10 and t 90. The t 10 curing time is the time to increase ΔS 10% above the minimum torque (ML). The t 10 curing time is known as scorch time to measure the processing safety during vulcanization. It should be long enough to avoid starting of vulcanization of the rubber before the mold is filled. Compared to the vulcanization of sulfur systems which have an introduction period due to time needed to form active reactive species, ETU and ZnO do not need to form any active species but directly undergo nucleophilic addition with allyl chloride on CR chain. Thus, CVCR has a high risk of scorch during processing. As shown in Table S1, the t 10 time of every DVCR is much longer than that of CVCR. An introduction period of 300–400 s is observed on each curve of DVCR before the curve upturn. These results prove that DVCR has better processing safety than CVCR. The longer t 10 time and introduction period may be attributed to the prior of reaction between acidic functional groups doped on N-CDs and MgO. When the acidic functional groups are consumed, the vulcanization proceeds rapidly. The t 90 time is used to indicate the optical curing time of the rubber. Although the t 90 time of each DVCR is higher than that of CVCR due to introduction time, all the DVCR samples can be crosslinked within 35 min.

The vulcanization kinetics could be computed by a differential equation related to time and temperature, regardless of the reaction mechanism [30,32–34]. The equation can be written as:
(3)
d α / d t = K
T
f
α
where α is the conversion, dα/dt is the vulcanization rate, t is the time, K is a kinetic constant at temperature T, and ƒ(α) is a function corresponding to the phenomenological model. When a curemeter is used to study the vulcanization kinetics, α should be defined as [35]:
α =
M t
−
M 0
M ∞
−
M 0
4
where M 0, M
t and M ∞ are the torque values at time zero, at a given curing time and at the end of the vulcanization process. The vulcanization process generally follows the manner of autocatalytic reaction, thus
f
( α )
is given as follows [30,34,36]:
f
α
=
α m
1 − α
n
( 5 )
where 0 ≤ m ≤ 1 and n ≥ 1 are both the orders of crosslinking reaction. m is the reaction order of autocatalytic reaction and n is the reaction order of non-autocatalytic reaction.

Thus, Eq. (3) can be expressed as follows:
(6)
d α / d t = K
T
α m
1 − α
n

As shown in Fig. 3(e), Eq. (6) can fit very well with the experimental data of DVCR. The coincidence of the fitted curve with the scatter plot of the experimental data and the fractioned values of m and n prove that the vulcanization of DVCR is not a simple elemental reaction but a series of simultaneous reactions undergo auto-catalyst process because of complex structure of N-CDs. The fitting parameters are listed in Table S2. It can be found that as the N-CDs loading increases, K decreases, which agrees with the conclusion in many other nanofiller containing systems [32,37,38].

The m values are lower than the n values, pointing out the dependency of the vulcanization reaction on the initial reactants of the compound. It indicates that the maximum rate is dependent on the initial state of vulcanization. The m + n values mainly show a decrease trend with the increase of N-CDs loading. These results indicate that the increase of N-CDs loading can raise the vulcanization rate at initial state and make the vulcanization of DVCR more like a non-autocatalyst reaction. Moreover, the addition of N-CDs loading raises the maximum vulcanization rate and shift it to earlier state.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dispersion state of N-CDs in DVCR
ce:section.ce:para: The dispersion of nanofillers in polymer matrix could influence the mechanical and other properties of composites. For DVCR, the N-CDs were compound into CR matrix by traditional method of melt blending. The SEM and TEM images of DVCR were used to investigate the dispersion of N-CDs in DVCR. Fig. 4 shows the SEM images of the freeze fractured surface of DVCR with various loadings of N-CDs. The aggregates of N-CDs are not observed even in DVCR-9, evidencing the good dispersion of N-CDs in DVCR. According to previous reports, the shining particles observed in all the SEM images are MgO particles due to the poor compatibility with CR. It can be found that with the increasing loading of N-CDs, the fractured surface of DVCR becomes smoother with smaller and less MgO particles. This result can be attributed to the reaction between MgO and acidic groups doped on N-CDs which increases as the loading of N-CDs increases. As shown in Fig. 5 , the TEM images further illustrate the good dispersion of N-CDs in CR matrix without aggregates of N-CDs on smaller scale.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The interfacial interaction and network structure of DVCR
ce:section.ce:para: N-CDs are not only chemical crosslinkers but also nanofillers which have strong interfacial interaction with CR. To investigate the reinforcement effect of N-CDs, the dynamic mechanical properties of DVCR and CVCR were measured by DMA analysis, as shown in Fig. 6 (a). It can be found that the storage modulus E′ of DVCR obviously increases with the increasing loading of N-CDs above the glass transition region. More interestingly, although CVCR has the highest crosslinking density, DVCR shows higher E′ than CVCR in the rubbery state. This strongly demonstrates the high reinforcement effect of N-CDs. Such high reinforcement effect can be attributed to the strong interfacial interaction between N-CDs and CR [10,18,39–41]. The interfacial interaction between N-CDs and CR includes not only the covalent crosslinking points but also the hydrogen bonds between the Cl atom and oxygen containing functional groups.

Rheological measurement at room temperature was used to analyze the storage (G′) and loss modulus (G″) at various frequencies in order to further illustrate network structure of DVCR compared to CVCR. As shown in Fig. S2, the CVCR exhibits typical characteristics of traditional permanently cross-linked polymer, owing to the frequency-independent nature of G′ and the great difference between G′ and G″. For DVCR, G′ of the composites with various N-CDs loadings is obviously higher than CVCR and increases gradually with the increase of N-CDs loading due to the stronger H-bonds at interfaces.

As shown in Fig. 6(b), the glassy transition temperature (T
g ) determined in terms of the peak value of tan δ is highest in CVCR, due to its highest crosslinking density. T
g of DVCR is lower, but it shifts to the high temperature as the loading of N-CDs of increases, meanwhile the peak height and area of tan δ decreases. These phenomena illustrate the chains of CR become more and more constrained due to increased crosslinking density and strong interfacial H-bonds at high loading of N-CDs [17].

The mechanical properties of rubbers are vital for the engineering applications. The stress-strain curves of DVCR-x are shown in Fig. 7 . From the stress-strain curves, the tensile strength, strain at break and modulus at 100% strain are shown in Table S3. As a kind of typical self-strengthening rubber, CVCR has a tensile stress of 24.4 MPa and an elongation at break of 1229%. In the DVCR composites, N-CDs play dual role of chemical crosslinkers and nanofillers with strong interfacial interaction, thus, DVCRs have better mechanical properties. For example, DVCR-6 has a tensile stress of 30.8 MPa, elongation at break of 1355% and the modulus at 100% strain of 5.3 MPa, these values are 26%, 10% and 39% higher than those of CVCR, respectively. However, the tensile strength of DVCR-9 becomes lower than DVCR-6, thus, too much N-CDs can reduce the mechanical properties of DVCR [42].

In summary, this paper reports a facile and green approach to prepare tough CR composites without traditional toxic curing agents by introducing N-CDs as both crosslinkers and nanofillers. A simple and environmentally friendly bulk high-temperature carbonization method was used to large-scale prepare nitrogen doped carbon nanodots. The abundant functional groups doped on the surface endow N-CDs with the dual role as chemical crosslinker and reinforcement nanofiller. The DVCR prepared by traditional melt blending method shows good processing safety and acceptable curing time. Except to covalent network formed by reaction between allyl chloride and amine groups, the interfacial H-bonds further constrain the mobility of CR chains. As a result, DVCR shows higher mechanical properties than CVCR. This work provides a new route to prepare green rubber materials with excellent mechanical properties.

Lingmin Kong: Conceptualization, Methodology, Visualization, Writing - original draft. Yong Zhu: Methodology, Investigation, Writing - review & editing. Guangsu Huang: Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Jinrong Wu: Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 51673120), Chengdu Science and Technology Bureau (2018-CY02-00028-GX), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.coco.2020.100441.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Sulfur vulcanization of hydrocarbon diene elastomers
Ind. Eng. Chem. Prod. Res. Dev.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M M.Coleman, J R. Shelton, J L. Koening, sulfur vulcanization of hydrocarbon diene elastomers, Industry & Engineering Chemistry Product Research and Development 13(3) (1974) 154-165. https://doi.org/10.1021/i360051a002.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Current issues for rubber crosslinking and its future trends of green chemistry strategy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G.G. Zhang, X.X. Zhou, Q.L. Zhang, Current issues for rubber crosslinking and its future trends of green chemistry strategy, Express Polymer Letters 13(5) (2019) 406-406. https://doi.org/10.3144/expresspolymlett.2019.33.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Preparation of zinc oxide free, transparent rubber nanocomposites using a layered double hydroxide filler

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Das, D.-Y. Wang, A. Leuteritz, K. Subramaniam, H.C. Greenwell, U. Wagenknecht, G. Heinrich, Preparation of zinc oxide free, transparent rubber nanocomposites using a layered double hydroxide filler, Journal of Materials Chemistry 21(20) (2011). https://doi.org/10.1039/c0jm03784b.
